content: It’s a common myth that the seasons happen because Earth gets closer or farther from the Sun, but that’s not true. The real reason is Earth’s tilt. Our planet is tilted about 23.5 degrees on its axis, and as it orbits the Sun, this tilt means different parts of Earth get more or less direct sunlight at different times of the year. When the Northern Hemisphere tilts toward the Sun, it experiences summer because sunlight hits it more directly and for longer periods. Six months later, when it tilts away, the sunlight is weaker and days are shorter, so it’s winter. The Southern Hemisphere experiences the opposite seasons at the same time. This tilt, not distance, controls the seasons, which explains why Earth is actually closest to the Sun in January, during Northern Hemisphere winter. It’s a simple, evidence-backed explanation that fits the facts better than the popular but mistaken “distance” idea.
